sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q1.1: This is true because a prograde burn adds orbital energy, which is exactly how spacecraft move to higher orbits.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q2.0: A forward burn adds energy and raises the orbit; it does not cause a drop to a lower orbit.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q2.2: The orbit first becomes elliptical, not immediately circular; circularization requires a second burn.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q2.3: Any velocity change in orbit immediately alters the trajectory; orbital mechanics always responds to burns.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q5.0: A retrograde burn removes orbital energy, which lowers the orbit rather than raising it.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q6.0: Burning at perigee raises the opposite side (apogee); the perigee itself stays essentially unchanged.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q6.2: A prograde burn at perigee raises apogee, making the orbit more elliptical, not more circular.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q6.3: Orbital changes take effect immediately along the trajectory; there is no delay until the next orbit.

sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q7.1: A retrograde burn would lower the orbit further from the 500 km target, not raise it.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q7.2: Radial burns are very inefficient for changing altitude; prograde burns are the standard method.
sp-sec9-u6-L1-Q7.3: Gravity alone cannot raise a satellite's orbit; an active engine burn is required.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q1.1: This is true because a Hohmann transfer consists of exactly two burns: one to enter the transfer ellipse and one to circularize.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q3.0: Hohmann transfers use the minimum fuel possible for a two-burn orbit change; they are the most efficient option.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q3.2: Hohmann transfers work between any two circular orbits at any altitude, not just LEO.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q3.3: Only 2 burns are required in a Hohmann transfer, not 4.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q5.1: This is true because the spacecraft coasts along the elliptical transfer path using no fuel between the two burns.

sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q6.0: Flying straight up wastes enormous delta-v fighting gravity directly and is not how orbital transfers work.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q6.2: Low-thrust spiral transfers take much longer and use more total delta-v than the efficient Hohmann method.
sp-sec9-u6-L2-Q6.3: Earth's gravity only pulls satellites downward; a rocket burn is required to raise an orbit.

sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q1.0: Speeding up raises your orbit and makes you move slower overall, causing you to fall further behind.

sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q2.0: A prograde burn would raise the orbit and slow you overall, making it harder to catch up.
sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q2.2: Orbital mechanics prevents natural slowing; without a maneuver, the gap remains constant forever.
sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q2.3: Lateral thrusters cannot change orbital altitude effectively; phasing requires altitude changes.

sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q5.0: Apollo 11 in 1969 performed lunar orbit rendezvous and docking, but Gemini 8 docked three years earlier.
sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q5.2: The Space Shuttle first flew in 1981, fifteen years after Gemini 8 achieved the first docking.
sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q5.3: The Soyuz-Apollo docking was in 1975, nine years after Gemini 8's first docking.

sp-sec9-u6-L3-Q6.1: This is true because modern Dragon capsules use autonomous systems with sensors and computers to dock with the ISS without human intervention.

sp-sec9-u7-L1-Q1.0: Solid rocket motors cannot be shut off or restarted because the fuel burns continuously until fully consumed.

sp-sec9-u7-L1-Q2.0: Liquid fuel is not necessarily cheaper; its advantage is precise controllability including throttle and restart.
sp-sec9-u7-L1-Q2.2: Liquid fuel does not inherently burn hotter; the key advantage is the ability to control, stop, and restart the engine.
sp-sec9-u7-L1-Q2.3: Both solid and liquid fuels need an oxidizer to burn; liquid fuel's advantage is throttle control.

sp-sec9-u7-L1-Q6.0: Solid boosters are actually heavier per unit of performance; their advantage is simplicity and high thrust.
sp-sec9-u7-L1-Q6.2: Liquid fuel works perfectly in the atmosphere and is used for first-stage engines during launch.
sp-sec9-u7-L1-Q6.3: Speed is not a meaningful comparison between fuel types; solid boosters provide high thrust, not speed.

sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q1.1: This is true because RP-1 is a highly purified form of kerosene specifically refined for rocket engines.

sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q2.0: Liquid hydrogen produces more thrust per kilogram of fuel (higher Isp), not RP-1.
sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q2.2: RP-1 is actually one of the densest rocket fuels; its advantage is density and ease of handling, not light weight.
sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q2.3: All chemical rocket fuels need an oxidizer (typically liquid oxygen) to combust.

sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q5.0: Cost is not the primary reason for choosing methane for Mars; the key is that it can be produced from Martian resources.
sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q5.1: Mars gravity has nothing to do with fuel choice; methane is chosen because it can be manufactured from local CO2 and water ice.
sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q5.3: While methane does burn cleanly, the primary reason for Mars missions is in-situ resource utilization, not cleanliness.

sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q7.1: Liquid hydrogen has the best Isp but is expensive to store cryogenically, conflicting with the low-cost requirement.
sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q7.2: Solid fuel cannot be throttled or restarted, limiting its usefulness for precise orbit insertion.
sp-sec9-u7-L2-Q7.3: Being newest does not make methane the best choice for frequent LEO flights; RP-1 is cheaper and proven.

sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q1.1: This is true because Isp is defined as the number of seconds one kilogram of fuel can produce one kilogram-force of thrust.

sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q2.0: An engine with Isp of 500 seconds is more efficient than one with 450 seconds, not less.
sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q2.2: An engine with Isp of 460 seconds is slightly more efficient than 450 seconds.
sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q2.3: An engine with Isp of 1,000 seconds would be far more efficient than 450 seconds.

sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q5.0: Lower Isp means worse fuel efficiency, so that engine would use more fuel, not less.
sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q5.1: Different Isp values mean different fuel consumption rates, so they cannot use the same amount.
sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q5.3: Isp directly measures fuel efficiency; it is not solely dependent on fuel type but also on engine design.

sp-sec9-u7-L3-Q7.1: This is true because ion engines accelerate ions electrically to achieve very high exhaust velocities with minimal propellant but generate only millinewtons of thrust.

sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q1.1: This is true because expendable rockets were the standard for decades, with each launch requiring a completely new vehicle.

sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q2.0: Reusability technology concepts existed, but surviving reentry and propulsive landing was the engineering challenge.
sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q2.1: Building new rockets every time is extremely expensive; the barrier was the engineering difficulty of landing and reuse.
sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q2.2: Rocket structures can withstand reuse; the challenge was the extra mass for landing hardware and fuel.

sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q3.0: The Space Shuttle actually proved the opposite: reusability only saves money when refurbishment is fast and cheap.

sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q5.0: Reusable rockets do not fly faster; the benefit is spreading the manufacturing cost across multiple flights.
sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q5.1: Reusability actually requires reserving some fuel for landing, so fuel per launch does not decrease.
sp-sec9-u8-L1-Q5.3: Reusable rockets sacrifice some payload capacity for landing hardware; they do not carry more.

sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q1.1: This is true because the boostback burn flips and redirects the booster back toward the landing zone after stage separation.

sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q3.0: Grid fins are aerodynamic surfaces and do not generate thrust; only engines produce thrust.
sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q3.1: Grid fins steer the booster through the atmosphere but do not shield anything from heat.
sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q3.3: Landing legs are a separate system; grid fins control atmospheric flight path, not leg deployment.

sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q5.0: Sea landings are not inherently cheaper; the reason is insufficient fuel for the boostback to return to land.
sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q5.2: Community safety is a consideration but not the primary reason for drone ship landings.
sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q5.3: Drone ships are stationary during landing; they do not move to catch boosters.

sp-sec9-u8-L2-Q6.0: Even at minimum throttle, the single Merlin engine produces more thrust than the nearly empty booster weighs, making hovering impossible.

sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q1.0: Launching to Mars at arbitrary times would waste enormous fuel because the planets would not be aligned for efficient transfer.

sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q2.0: Every 6 months is too frequent; Mars and Earth take about 26 months to realign for an efficient transfer.
sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q2.1: Every 12 months is too frequent; the synodic period of Earth and Mars is approximately 26 months.
sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q2.3: Every 5 years is too infrequent; Mars launch windows actually occur about every 26 months.

sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q5.0: Two weeks is far too short for a trip covering hundreds of millions of kilometers via a transfer orbit.
sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q5.1: Two months is too short; the Hohmann transfer ellipse to Mars takes 7-9 months to traverse.
sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q5.3: Three years is too long for a Hohmann transfer; faster trajectories exist, and the standard one takes 7-9 months.

sp-sec9-u9-L1-Q6.0: Straight-line paths are impossible in orbital mechanics; spacecraft must follow curved trajectories governed by gravity.

sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q1.1: This is true because the spacecraft borrows kinetic energy from the planet's orbital motion, requiring no onboard fuel.

sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q2.0: The spacecraft's engines are not involved in a gravity assist; the speed change comes entirely from gravitational interaction.
sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q2.1: Solar wind is far too weak to significantly alter a spacecraft's trajectory.
sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q2.3: Planetary magnetic fields have negligible effect on spacecraft trajectories compared to gravitational interaction.

sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q4.0: Ten years is far too frequent; this specific alignment of four outer planets is extremely rare.
sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q4.1: Fifty years is still too frequent for the four-planet Grand Tour alignment that Voyager exploited.
sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q4.3: One thousand years is too rare; the alignment specifically occurs every 175 years.

sp-sec9-u9-L2-Q5.0: Gravity assists can both speed up and slow down spacecraft depending on the approach direction relative to the planet's motion.

sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q1.0: GPS satellites orbit at 20,200 km and only cover Earth; they cannot reach probes millions of kilometers away.

sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q2.0: No network of satellites orbits other planets for this purpose; tracking is done from Earth-based antennas.
sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q2.2: Laser ranging is used for the Moon but not routinely for deep space probes; radio is the standard.
sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q2.3: Star cameras help with orientation but cannot determine precise position; DSN radio tracking does that.

sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q5.0: Mars rovers do have steering; the issue is the signal delay makes real-time control impossible.
sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q5.1: Mars is not too far for radio signals to reach; signals do arrive, just with a significant delay.
sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q5.3: DSN antennas are fully bidirectional; they both send commands and receive data.

sp-sec9-u9-L3-Q6.1: This is true because both Voyager probes maintain contact with Earth via NASA's DSN 70-meter antennas despite being over 20 billion km away.

sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q1.0: The first law explains coasting in space, not the mechanism by which exhaust creates thrust.
sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q1.1: The second law relates force, mass, and acceleration but does not explain action-reaction thrust generation.
sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q1.3: The law of gravitation describes gravitational attraction, not the principle behind rocket propulsion.

sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q2.1: This is true because discarding empty fuel tanks reduces dead weight, allowing remaining fuel to accelerate the lighter rocket more efficiently.

sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q5.0: A ratio of 0.5 means thrust is only half the weight, so the rocket cannot overcome gravity.
sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q5.1: A ratio of 0.8 means gravity still exceeds thrust, keeping the rocket on the pad.
sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q5.3: While a ratio of 2.0 would work, the minimum required for liftoff is exactly 1.0.

sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q7.1: Newton's second law says no force means zero acceleration, but the first law specifically describes constant motion without force.
sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q7.2: The third law involves paired forces; once engines are off, there is no ongoing action-reaction pair.
sp-sec9-u10-L1-Q7.3: Kepler's first law describes orbital shapes; this scenario describes straight-line motion in deep space.

sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q1.0: One burn would only raise one side of the orbit, creating an ellipse rather than reaching a new circular orbit.
sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q1.2: Three burns are not needed for a standard Hohmann transfer; only two are required.
sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q1.3: Four burns would be excessive; the standard Hohmann transfer is optimized at exactly two burns.

sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q2.0: Speeding up raises your orbit and paradoxically makes you move slower overall, causing you to fall further behind.

sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q5.0: Fuel type affects costs somewhat, but the key difference is turnaround time and refurbishment effort between flights.
sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q5.1: Rocket size is not the primary factor; the Shuttle was large and reusable but very expensive per flight.
sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q5.3: SpaceX does carry astronauts on Crew Dragon; reusability savings apply regardless of payload type.

sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q7.1: RP-1 cannot be manufactured on Mars, which disqualifies it for a mission requiring Mars-based refueling.
sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q7.2: Liquid hydrogen has the highest Isp but cannot be easily produced or stored on Mars.
sp-sec9-u10-L2-Q7.3: Solid fuel cannot be throttled, restarted, or manufactured on Mars from local resources.
